head.title: Haas Notebook 11.000 Haas Notebook 11.001 Haas Notebook 11.002 Haas Notebook 11.003 Haas Notebook 11.004 Haas Notebook 11.005 Haas Notebook 11.006 Haas Notebook 11.007 Haas Notebook 11.008 Notebook 11.018 Haas Notebook 11.019 Haas Notebook 11.020 Haas Notebook 11.021 Haas Notebook 11.022 Haas Notebook 11.023 Haas Notebook 11.024 Haas Notebook 11.025 Haas Notebook 11.026 Haas Notebook 11.065 Haas Notebook 11.066 Haas Notebook 11.067 Haas Notebook 11.068 Haas Notebook 11.069 Haas Notebook 11.070 Haas Notebook 11.071 Haas Notebook 11.072 Haas Notebook 11.073 Haas Notebook 11.074 Haas Notebook 11.075 Haas Notebook 11.076 Haas Notebook 11.077 Haas Notebook 11.078 Haas Notebook 11.079 Haas Notebook 11.080 Haas Notebook 11.081 Haas Notebook 11.082 Haas Notebook 11.083 Haas Notebook 11.084 Haas Notebook 11.085 Haas Notebook 11.086 Haas Notebook 11.087 Haas Notebook 11.088 Haas Notebook 11.089 Haas Notebook 11.090 Haas Notebook 11.091 Haas Notebook 11.092 Haas Notebook 11.093 Haas Notebook 11.094 Haas Notebook 11.095 Haas Notebook 11.096 Haas Notebook 11.097 Haas Notebook 11.098 Haas Notebook 11.099 Haas Notebook 11.100 Haas Notebook 11.101 Haas Notebook 11.102 Haas Notebook 11.103 Haas Notebook 11.104 Haas Notebook 11.105 Haas Notebook 11.106 Haas Notebook 11.107 Haas Notebook 11.108 Haas Notebook 11.109 Haas Notebook 11.110 Haas Notebook 11.111 Haas Notebook 11.112 Haas Notebook 11.113 Haas Notebook 11.114 Haas Notebook 11.115 Haas Notebook 11.116 Haas Notebook 11.117 Haas Notebook 11.118 Haas Notebook 11.119 Haas Notebook 11.119-0 Haas Notebook 11.119A Haas Notebook 11.120 Haas Notebook 11.121 Haas Notebook 11.122 Haas Notebook 11.123 Haas Notebook 11.124 Haas Notebook 11.125 Haas Notebook 11.126 Haas Notebook 11.127 Haas Notebook 11.128 Haas Notebook 11.129 Haas Notebook 11.130 Haas Notebook 11.131 Haas Notebook 11.132 Haas Notebook 11.133 Haas Notebook 11.134 Haas Notebook 11.135 Haas Notebook 11.136 Haas Notebook 11.137 Haas Notebook 11.138 Haas Notebook 11.139 Haas Notebook 11.140 Haas Notebook 11.141 Haas Notebook 11.142 Haas Notebook 11.143 Haas Notebook 11.144 Haas Notebook 11.145 Haas Notebook 11.146 Haas Notebook 11.147 Haas Notebook 11.148 Haas Notebook 11.149 Haas Notebook 11.150 Haas Notebook 11.151 Haas Notebook 11.152 Haas Notebook 11.153 Haas Notebook 11.154 Haas Notebook 11.155 Haas Notebook 11.156 Haas Notebook 11.157 Haas Notebook 11.158 Haas Notebook 11.159 Haas Notebook 11.160 Haas Notebook 11.161 Haas Notebook 11.162 Haas Notebook 11.163 Haas Notebook 11.164 Haas Notebook 11.165 Haas Notebook 11.166 Haas Notebook 11.167 Haas Notebook 11.168 Haas Notebook 11.169 Haas Notebook 11.170 Haas Notebook 11.171 Haas Notebook 11.172 Haas Notebook 11.173 Haas Notebook 11.174 Haas Notebook 11.175 Haas Notebook 11.176 Haas Notebook 11.177 Haas Notebook 11.178 Haas Notebook 11.179 Haas Notebook 11.180 Haas Notebook 11.181 Haas Notebook 11.182 Haas Notebook 11.183 Haas Notebook 11.184 Haas Notebook 11.185 Haas Notebook 11.186 Haas Notebook 11.187 Haas Notebook 11.188 Haas Notebook 11.189 Haas Notebook 11.190 Haas Notebook 11.191 Haas Notebook 11.192 Haas Notebook 11.193 Haas Notebook 11.194

div.p: papata aa´xkini = I fall many times
pǎ´˳ta peta = marsh, slough; cove, bay, inlet, pond
wi´ci păta = marsh ( for rice ), cove,bay, inlet, swamp, pond ( v & j )
wi´ci pătan u´kini = marsh, rather round than long?
omaxka pătac = ( f ) crocdile ( alligator ) swamp
pătac = Stream of the Cranes (Coulée des Grues) goes to Old River and from there to Red River
pă´tac = in the lowlands, in the marshes
wici pǎtan uxkăhă = there is no water pond ( J ) ; Gatsch. gives this in a slightly different form as the equilavent of " vuphar, thistle-down, etc," but this certainly wrong
pehu = to dress wood ( with an ax, etc. )
pe´hukatani = I dress wood (as to square up a sill)
ri´xku pe´huni = piece of wood dressed with the hatchet
a´uc mi´li hopărîkati = Red River overflows
hopi´ta = they go out ( of the water )
u´ni ta´-icu pi´xkatan = to blood someone
titixk ta´ucăna ho´pinu´ra = outlet, mouth " bayou's going out into a lake "
ci´kuri he´ku pin la´pu = this knife is well sharpened
ti´ni tikpi´xsis = an implement for making sharp points on the sugar cane ( S )
tinipi´xsi = arrow-head point ( G ) something you sharpen with a knife ( V )
pihu pio = to conceal or hide oneself
pi´xsakatan = I am pressing or squeezing something
ro´hina ta´hina ta po´lun hek pixtci = this ink-well is full
ri´xku-mǎra ( ku ) wi´xci pi´xtci = a barrel full of water
ka´i pixtc = bile gall, " full of yellow " ( G ) ( prob. wrong, J )
(uk) pi´xton yayeki = he is almost mad
ro´puxtin tu´xs tapi´ni o´xtini = the man who owns a cotton-gin
pi´ra pi´ran = to finish, accomplish, eud, complete, become
unkpi´ragi = he became " whiskey making tree "
o´ka tuxk ni´xsa upi´ratu = a child being born, or that has just been born
on u´xpiraha taxki = rib " a flat sided human bone "
atapă´ra = this thing is flat, level ( like a table )
ta´xkuk ha´ria ta´rixtc ata´păra = this tree is as high as the house, on a level with the house
ta´-uci (or tawic ) u'pic pa´nu = water is too much disturbed
ho´xtci ku´ cha'n la'lu pi´taku = although he is lame, he walks well
ka´hi tapi´ta = scaffold, " floor to walk on"
o´ni uxcka pituxki = trail of man
pi´ta ta´ witci = a ladder to go up by; staircase ( V)
pi´ta ta kaxtini = steps of a ladder
ti´hika pi´ti = next year, " next summer "
ta´xkic po´kuni = a skin covering ( J + V)
po´xtu pu´xtu = to cut wood across ( the grain )
tira no´kin po´xtukani = I am cutting cloth, a yard measure
wu´lin po´xtu = cut on the bias, oblique
ci´kur ta´kaxpunta puxtukani = I cut with scissors
o´rwa a´xcuk ta ponane´ = a New Year's greeting, has some reference to some kind of big day
karictîk ikpo´ku = he looks toward me
ta´xtcak ta po´ = a watch, clock
un u´xtcuxsu tăxcpa sa(xk) ta po´ = cross-eyed
apa´xkal ta po = one who sees double
po´n na = Look you! (expt. of atonishment) Let us look! (v)
hăya ta´pu tǎhasa´ = the mirror relects
ha-ickăra po´kan = I bend backward, evidently, " I see upward" (in bending over backward)
ho´xka ta-po´ = a hole to look through
uni po-o´kini = I stop someone, prob. " I have spoken to someone to stop him"
pûxtcū = to crackle, crack, burst, break wind
ta´cki pu´xtcu = the breaking of wind
wi´xsi ti´pudja = the foam on water
ri´xku ti´pudjexka´ra = the or sprouts on a tree (G) ( Johnson says " to pass by a tree", evidently deriving the vb. from he'xku) ( ? )
puxti = to open itself, to break out, unfold
pu´na = soft rd. obj. , bull, india-rubber pincushion
huxkapu´ri = corn mush (puri always occurs withh prceding complement)
cixkal purkitan = soft or brittle stone
puska = to swell, inflate or distend by blowing
puskakatani = I cause something to swell
u´cki tawo´lon puskaka´tan = I distend a vessel
u´djik ta puska´ni = belly swollen, (other) vessels
puyuxkata´ni = I shake something (as a tree)
ta ti´rak rixtco´ra = tie for curtains, etc.
wixci ticte´mir ra´ = "hard water vapor", steam
nu'xtci te´'tini´yura = virgin, "woman with a hard or firm heart"
wi´xci ha´ra a´ki = the water is hardened, i.e. frozen
ra´ha = to comb, also to paint; comb (m.)
ka´nak ra´hi kat = What is this noise
rax raxpa = to enclose, to catch by enclosing
icki ikraxkina´ki = I am constipated, "backsides to me closed"
nu´xtci o´ka ra´xkoki = the woman bears a child
oka tuk a´sak ra´xku = twins, "children borne together"
nu´xtci raxko´gi = woman who has had a child
pu´na raxpi´laxkini = I am beginning to play ball
u´cik ra´xpa = the spleen, milt, "spoon flat thing" (f.)
tu´xcik ra´xpagu = pick-ax, flat spoon (G.; V. & J. do recognize this)
i-axka´tan uxcira´xpatan = I work with a hoe
o'katuk o´xsinta ugra´spuni ta o´xsin ukra´xspuni = I flatten the head of a child
tacka raxpu´ni = leggings (of buckskin) used in old times
ikraxpun di´ra ka´xtigani = I button my coat
i´graxpun ti´ra waxci´laxkini = I take off my coat
i´graxpun ti´ra i´rilaxkini = I put on my coat
ta´-i ka´ntuk ră´xtagu = the fire crackles
rămani = I flash (a promise) with a torch
rămakani = I am going with a torch
a´-i ta răma = torch (used in hunting)
ura´naxkini = I have brought him (a child) up (non-reverential form)
yugmu´xku ra´ni = (a place) grown over with grass
ha´li ran wăka = a place spoiled by being grown over with grass and bushes
ranna = ropes used to fasten down the lower part of a tent (J.)
ăxcka ăraji = my leg from below
ha´xka tă racki ra´xckani = corn-husks, the chaff of corn, fodder
ki ugra´-uni = I put it inside of (the earth)
e´pu-ra´-u = to bend and place there
ri´ki too too (usually indicating space relations)
ri´kăhă less less (or the word nearest less in Tun.)
ta´ri the house n ro´χpanda ri´kin adv
ha´riki as many as many (It has the same meaning as hi´naχku le´hi, cf.
o´ni n yu´ru adj ri´χk o´ni n naχk a tall man in the woods like a human being, man of the woods, a mythic being seen in the woods
ta´χku - meli nic Baton Rouge, La.
tati´rak riχtco´ra tie for curtains, etc.
ri´χtcu tca´χka maⁿ´ku ri´χtcu o´ni me´li ta sa´pa hi´na ya´ siksa´pakata = having planted 4 wooden pickets to whip a negro, they whipped him
ropuχtin ta´ riχtcu cord to fasten bales of cotton
frizzly hair like that of negroes

tr.td: cord to fasten bales of cotton

div.p: riχk' e´lu ri´pin = tree with plenty of fruit or acorns
Creole bois de fer; (Gatschet has " laurel tree ")
a´la ha´yi ri´χpi canebrake a canebrake (G.)
" much old cane ", " a lot of cane put up in an open space " (V.)
roχa to gig, roha to gig
e´niχku aroχparoχpa´ndä a´taci = they go triple-file
o´cka tadjara ro´χpa feathers on legs footweb (Gatschet)
se´ti ro´χpand their relations their relations (V.)
ci´χputa-ira ro´χtu kan ho´tu kan ka´na) = different kinds of cream-colored beads?
ruina´ the heat, ru´ina the heat the heat (J.)
yanic ru´kaka´taⁿ to conduct or lead cattle
u´ni ta rukasaⁿ´ an ugly person
ta ci´χkal ru´χkic on this side of the stone

tr.td: on this side of the stone

div.p: irupakati = (f) I have the diarrhea (names of diseases are feminine)
rupu rupo rapu = to sleep, sleep; used in speaking of death; also instead of a´xcuk, day, to indicate a period 24 hours
ăronasa = they (people) did not know him (e´rusa to know)
ta mi´nu taxk ho´yic u´xki ho´nussa = the cat climbs down from the tree (stem honu)
sa´ha te´ti = galaxy, "dog trail" (G. says te´ti rowa "white trail")
ha uksa´ni = I threaten to strike him
să´yoxta să-yo´x̣ta sai-o´xta săyoxtax̣tc = a necklace composed of silver pieces ([?]. and J.), Gatschet says "bone beads" but appears to be wrong
e´x̣si-saho´kᵘ = my stepfather (G.), my aunt's husband ([?])
igatci-sa´hu = my stepmother (G.), my mother's sister ([?])
sa´kura = eating, possibly also the name for an animal
sa´guna = we let us eat (we is an exclusive)
ti´ra saxtci = to wash cloth, clothing
e´xtira saxcigani = I wash my shirt
saxtcuta´ni = I paste or glue; I screw, I put together
ta sa´xtcun = paste, glue, to put the button there
sa´ma = to be ripe, to cook
ha´li sa´man pa´xkali = a brick kiln
ha´li sa´-man ha´namoni = (m.) a pile of bricks
sa´maxkatani = I am making a roast, or I am cooking something
asa´nisi´na = they 2f. bet with one another
ho uksa´niwi = he wagered himself to the alligator outside
sa´pa sa´r͑pa săpa = to whip, lick; a switch
ta sapa tu = a little whip
sa´pa = to cool, become cold, cold
si´pi = cold (seem to have become slightly differentiated from one root)
i´mataⁿ sa´paxtani = I cool myself down; (and "let me brag of myself")
sa´patăhă´kixtci´ = when they have not cooled (the fever) off
u´wi uksi´pin wa´nan = who has chills
iksi´pilaki´ = I am cold, become chilled
iksi´pikata = I tremble with cold or shiver
wi´ci sa´pi = waves in the water
o´rwa ha´xci tasa´ra = "a Frenchman who prays"
ta sa´ru = to girdle, belt, band
e´cku sadjipa´ha = there is no rain
e´cku sadji ko´rini = a tempest, stormy weather, "an assemblage of rain storms"
ta´xkic ta sa´yun = to stretch a hide, or a stretched or drawn hide
săha ta po = a glass; a fortune teller
ti´cu tale´xni săhata = sash of a window
a-i ta wăhaⁿ săhaxta ka´li = lamp
se´hikati = until the day, day breaks ([?])
tikse´hiti = until the day dawned for her
se´pi = thin, lean, poor in flesh
si, sa n root of most plural pron.
e´stuku = my paternal uncle, my father's youngest brother
are on the authority of Gatschet,
but Oolsine + Johnson did not understand
" I move somthing back into place ", and
while they could make nothing of the other 2)
sihurhi´laxkini = I have begun to dry up
ka´ntu si´hun-o´nta = they have dried some things
tex skala = the escalin or bit
ikso´lu la´pu yatăhă´ ka´han paha´ = if I am treated + not cured there is no payment
ta´ ya´xci sin ta´xsin so´lu = the treatments of female maladies
rixku o´raeki so´pun = a leaf withered by dryness
ta´xtcik ta po ta so´win = swinging of a pendulum
ta´-uki ta´nuxtin sowi´xkatan = I swing something
ri´xku oxsta´yi = trunk of a tree as far as the branches, body of the tree
ri´xkixtc aa´xkintac = I have or had been strong (J.), something coming behind that is very strong (V.)
says " a stick with many prongs "
ca´xka(ni) = an old Indian game in which two marked pieces of wood were shuffled up in cotton + bets made on which sides they would have uppermost
căpa = to be satisfied, pleased, to thank
căpo´wita = thanks! you made me glad (V.)
ucă´patani = I thank him; I have made him glad (V.)
wica´ri lehi´ = thou hast indeed time
ca´ukati = the rapids in Red Rive in Alexandria, fn.
ce´lama că´lama = afflicted, pained; unfortunates, miserable ones
ukcă´lama wiagi´ = that pained him; hes was sad (V.)
îcê´ruka î´cêrukaki = I strain at something
ci = a male human being, man
o´ni ci´hayi tu = a little old man
ci´ oka tuxk = a male infant, a little boy (V.)
ta c o´wi = the sweat of a man or human being
nuxtci ticuyi = married woman "woman her husband"
ta yaxci´ku ukcixkaka´tan = I succor a sick person
ci´xkani = to aid // I aid someone
inte´lia'ktc ta tixcixkani´ = to deliver the soul of the dead; we save our souls (V.)
cî´xkal maito´ro ukho´xkakan = I make a hole thru a rock
cî´xkal-pa´xka l = Rock-pilers, the Natchez Indians
kotita´pa ci´xpari f = kidney beans; lima beans (J.)
cixpu = to prick or sting; to point; to fasten (ef. tcuxpu)
ci´la cî´xpu = pain or stitches in the side (?)
(seems to mean " the sting or bite of some animal ")
cî´xpu ta i´ra = big round silver pieces worn on breast
sa uk cixpika´tani = to set a dog on one
ci´xtika = poison, venom, what disturbs one; to fast, to mourn
(perhaps the last meanings are connected with the fact that poisonous herbs were taken at such times)
in one place as " maple ", in another as " mayhall tree ", and by Johnson as " pond bush "
cixtika yakani = I fast, I take poison (i.e, medicine)
cî´la = creature, animal, "small cattle", used for caterpillar
cila ta ko´ra = fern, "animal drink" (Ind. medicine)
' ' ro´wa = "white creature", a white worm living in the ground
cîlawă´ya = tanager or summer red bird;
Creole cai or cai jaune; Gat.
ci´luka = screech owl (has a cry like a little chicken, feathery ears, 4-5 in., flies at night
wicămi wan ya´wi = he wants to play (J.)
aci´mukani aci´muxani = I am eating several kinds of food together, as bread and meat, or I am eating bread and something with it (J.)
cîra (see cêru) = to tremble with fear, fever, etc., to fear
tănêko'la ci'rïkan = I smell a flower
cka ckan = (f.) (m.); foot, leg (?), root, stalk, pole
ri´xku ko´ra tăxcka = wheel of wagon
cil o´cka ra = (m.) an insect with hard legs
cki = posteriors, buttocks of man (males) and animals
urua´xka co´ku = pounded or crushed rice, pounding rice in a mortar (V.)
o´ni ucra´ = a man who can keep his own council and not tell things to everyone (V.), a discreet man
ri´xku o´xcta = a green china ball tree (J.)
taxcta´xpuc to´lu = the oval of the face, my face is round
ko´xsuk uxcta´djuli = the antennae of the crab, "crab's eyelids"
îsta´xpu la´xpa = spots on my face
he´ sa´gunictu´kaha = I cannot eat that
icarêhă´ a´xsani ctu´kaha = I have not time to come
ta cuala´ka = a blackguard, a man with bad ways
o´ni ta cuala´ka = a bad boy, person (J.)
ta nixsara´ku uka´la hayile´yuta cuitci´wi = this boy has shot his arrow straight up
ha´-acucuitcun hacucutcun = maynard's or wild apple, Creole aconu
ta ckala´xpi ta cu´xtci = a shoemaker
ixcu´lihi´laki = I begin to warm up
ri´xku o´rêcki cu´matc = China tree (the proper name)
cū´mûla = a red-headed lizard (?), or something similar (V.)
hu´xtcu tape´ = commode, chamber-pot, "to catch urine" (?)
uktaphe´ni = I salute him by shaking hands
yu´nka ta´rani = the cord is slender
ro´wa ta´ran = a sail, a flag (V.)
e´yu tăxkatani´ = I extend my arms
ta´xkani = fold of skin under lizard's chin that moves when he turns head
tci´ya-tata´xkkani = (m.) lizard (tci´ya refers to its jumping; ta´xkani as above)
an island or isle; that which is surrounded by something, as land by water, as the wood of a handle in the eye of an ax, by the iron of the ax (By.)
tăxkar u´kikin = wide in some places
o´taracpa = his shoulder-blade (of man, ox, hog, etc.)
ta´sayi titixki´ku = Bayou Choctaw, or Bayou de l'Île (at the eastern end of Bayou Lamourie, and so called because it runs around 2 islands)
tă´cpa = noun probably, "at the side, at one side"
ini yu´ru tăcpa sa = canine teeth
un u´xcutuxsu tăxepa saxk tapo´ = cross-eyed ?
tăcpa sa´xku ra-u = to put on one side, as if fixing onself up on only one side
e´xsi ta´tek = father's brother, my old uncle
tă´ya = overflowing, in surplussage, beyond, more
tihika´ midj ili´ tăya tu´xkusa = 9 years more than 20
tēh = to turn about, undulate, circle
teh u´wa = he goes circling, he wanders
ri´xku te´xkelu tu´xku = a fig tree
te´xpi = patched places, to patch, things patched by placing their ends together
sîkhē´ri atē´xpitak = their canoes joined end to end, i.e. like rafters
te´lia = soul, shade, reflection; shade of a tree
te´lin kua´tu = (a blue bird which is blue on the back but yellow on the belly; it comes in the spring -- G.) this is probably a mistake for me´lin, the full form being o´xcta me´lin
la´spi ko´dju te´nikati = the sounding or ringing of the bell
la´spi ko´dju tenin axki´ = I have rung a bell
tē´pi n tē´pi ni = articulation, knuckle
ta´xcka te´pini yu´ru = a long leg
la´xpa te´pinto = silver ornaments held or jointed at one end
ti ta = appears to be sign of plural
e´ti ko´ri = family, all my company
on' itikaha´ = I hate this person
ti´hika pi´ti = next year, in the early spring (V.)
tîk = toward, in the direction of
kan ha´lukinîctîk wi´ya = Into what villages are you going?
ta´-uc mî´lictik ani´ = I'm going toward Red River
karictîk ikpo´ku = heh looks toward me
ta´xsapaxctîk = the side toward the north, northward, north
kalăranistîk = toward the place where I am standing
ta´xtci ti´xpulak ho´nwa = meteor, shooting star
ticuki ha´xtani = I open the way
ti´xcu maito´ro ra´u = the threshhold of the door, "placed across the door," cross-piece on a door
ta´ric ti´mura = ridge of nose; ridge of house (ri) (V.)
tī´ni = dinner (>Fr. le dîner), to dine
tax cî´kur he´ku ti´ni la´pu = this knife cuts well
haxkati´ni = fine hominy, "cut corn" (?), Creole petit gru
tinipi´xsi = arrow-head (G.); something one sharpens with a knife (V.)
pu´n' e´xtin = I have a ball
pu´na îkaha = I do not have a ball
puna extin ya´nikaxtca = I will have a ball
tī´ric = before, in front, forward, ahead
to´nik hîtuak ka´n o´tisan = What is the name of the man who is passing?
ticki tohon = hominy with something put into it, hominy cooked with a hog's head, soup
ya´nic tiski-tuhu´ = something put with beef, soup
taucăna tixckira = given by Gatschet at "the bottom of the lake" but probably "something lying in a lake" would be more correct
a´i wo tcu´ra ticte´nir = smoke-hole, chimney hole
ri ticte´nir ko´xpa = partition wall of a house
titiku ka´yi = Yellow Bayou (near the Atchafalaya)
pixti ti´tik = Bayou Lamourie (G.), Bayou de La Source (V. & G.); might be applied to any bayou (J.)
ta to´ tohokati = it rains and turns to sleet, it sleets (V.)
torati´ni = an icicle (V.), a coasting of very slippery ice (G.)
nirwäkata´ to´ho = it is necessary to throw on the cemetery; to put something in a grave
ticki toho´n = to put something into hominy, hominy with something else put into it
tōx = to go off, explore (as a gun)
wi´cki tō´xkatan = I fire a gun (with explosion)
cixkal tōxkatan = soft or brittle stone (J.)
tô´xka tcu´wi = wood duck, Creole canard branchu
tô´xka tăn = mallard duck; Creole canard français
to´ka ka´wa tu = "little diver", a diving bird of medium size, the male a deep purple with white head, the female gray; probably the widgeon (G.)
to´ka ka´wa tă = "great diver", probably the loon (G.)
hayayai´ îkto´xkuni = I am fatigued, I'm getting tired
ayî îkto´xku a´ki = I have gotten tired
Gatschet has to´xkura or to´xkuraxtci which he translates " the cousins or poor relations," but he is evidently wrong.
taxcta´xpuc to´lu = my cheek, my face roundness
ixstaxpuc to´lu = "the oval of the face"
ri´xku koato´luka ma´xsaxk = prismatic wood with six faces (G.), six round sticks (J.)
ri´xku ti´xsiku = prismatic wood with 8 faces (G.), eith round sticks
(In these 2 last examples we should perhaps read korato´luka, or perhaps ko is only a repetition of the preceding -ku)
tōmū = to pound, mash, beat, crush
tî´cukîc u´witan tu´muwi = he hurls himself or bumps against the door
ai to´ra = charcoal (E.) (perhaps this is ta o´ra)
wig wi´xci to´raxta = a cask of wine
ixkin tuxro´xpani = 3rd fing. touching the little fing. "
tu´il ukalixta´ni = I bend something, I plant it leaning over
e´tukumaciku = father-in-law, father of wife or husband, step-father (?V.)
tu´xura = up on a hill, a hillside or bank (f. timura)
o´n e´nixku săstai tu´pa = 3 stout men
tu´paha tu´ = not at all thin
ū´a = the great horned owl; Creole hibon groose tête
rixku tcexki´ ta-u´k = wooden chair to sit upon
taya´nic si´nima ta´xuric sinukta´ni = I put the cows in the corral
ri ukina = inhabitant, let us go into the house
ta´ri ta´-ukîe = the dwelling, the house for sitting down
ha-uki´ni = I am going to sit down
ta´xkiri ha-u´kin = a blot, "dirt stain"; a spot of dirt (J.)
to´roa ta´hal u´kinic = at New Orleans
tcu-ukani = I watch (in speaking of the dead, being alone to watch)
ukukinu´xki = he had put him (in a house to fast)
a´la tu´xsu la´pu = a good ball for a gun
u´xcik ta´kirka = oyster, "the mollusk its shell"
u´cîk ra´xpa = a hoe (J.), the spleen, milt (G.), "spoon flat"?
tu´xcik ra´xpagu = probably the same as above, pickax (G.)
un on u´ni o´ni = a person, persons, people, men
ūrux = to whinny like a horse or crow like a cock
ta s'te´ku u´ruxkuta = the horse whinnies, neighs
ta ka´paciku u´ruxkuta = the cock crows
udjăxka o´nhai = an old not lively toad, perhaps "frog"
ăp´ho-ugwi´yani = I raise and throw away the cover
kiwiyon-wanyati = she wanted to throw him away
waha = to weep, cry, to crow, also the bellowing of a bull, singing of a bird
ta sa wohugu = the dog barks (?)
wăha = to light (as a fire) (cf. han)
wă´han pa´ha = there is no more brightness
yu´nka a´-i taweha´ni = wick of lamp
să´ma o´ni lu´pi wăhanda-ka´ha = they do not light up a dead person
ama´yi wăhasak = on each (bank), opposite each other; open on each side (V.)
ta ci´xkal wăsak wă´hasak = opposite the stone, opposite an open place with a stone on it
te´ti wă´hani tu' = path, the turning row in a field
wăhătăhă wăhatăhă = often, each time, or moment
la´tika a´ha wa´ka = the night will never come, it will never be night
tun he´ku ya´kawî´kaha wa´ka = this man will never come or attend
sigwa´kawi = he has ordained or commanded them
sigwa´ka = their custom; they have been commanded
ni´ri wă´ka = a cemetery (?), "a decayed hole"; "a grave that has cave in" (V.)
ha´li ran wă´ka = land spoiled by being run over with bushes, etc.
wa´xka = to break up; rough to the touch
a´mar waxkikaxtca = you will break off enough
urua´xka co´ku = pounded or crushed rice
wa´xkuni = I close up between legs (?)
ta´häl-we´ri ma-i ukwă´xsani = I jump across the trench
ta´-uc kiwăxsan = I jump into the water
hawă´xsa tiku´kiwi = springing upward after the arrow he overtook it (he sat on it)
ăcka sa´xku wăssata = I am hopping on one leg
tiric tco´ha ta we´sa = dance leader (J.)
po´luxk le´xpi ta wa´xtcu = "to pull out stopper from bottle", corkscrew
igra´xpun ti´ra wa´xtcuni = I take off my coat
o´ni u´ni uwa´xtconta = to pull out a person's tooth
kanhia´ti hîn´ wa´lik = Why do you call?
kanhia´ti-xtc igwa´liki = Why dost thou call me?
wana = to wish, desire; to burn
ya´xci wa´nan = feverish, one sick of the stone, one who has a fever
ro´hina wa´nakatani = I have burned some paper
ha´-ici ra te´lia ta´wanon = the burner of souls; applied 1) to God as punisher, 2) to the devil
u´wi uksi´pin wa´nan = who has chills
ya´xci wanan tan = a great fever (illness)
ta ni´cara wa´nan = inflammation of the stomach (V.); wanting a young person (J.)
wa´ntahă wan´taha´ = ancient times, old times, centuries back
wa´ntaha o´ni să´ma = the ancient Indians or men
wǎrani tako´ra = a polypod which grows on bark of greenwood, moss (?), "hunting medicine or drink"
ick i he´nulixtc cu´xtci wa´rok = my buckskin trousers are ripped along the seams
wa´roro te´xponnaxk yu´kawi = he went half a mile, or he got half a mile
ha´lukin na´kewatci = the war of a country
wan´taha´ on ta´watci = warrior of ancient times
sîgwa´taxkuta´ = they are concealed through him
ta cila´ ta´watanîku = "the beast that conceals"
na´ka-ta´watani = (m.) "concealer of the army," a beetle or bug
wato´ruk = the little blue heron; Creole un gru
wayi = to be economical, to be careful, pay attention to something
wa´yikani = I do not waste, I am economical
wa´yitux ya´kani = I pay attention, I come slowly, i.e. carefully
we´ri = to dig up, to rummage
titik we´ri = a canal, a ditch occupied by a bayou
wik- wi- = obj. pref.; poss. pref.
u-ix mi´ku cakn = Curesed! Confounded! (cré maudit)
wī = to hear, to listen, to understand
imata´n wīhūka´tan = I fan myself (all the time)
wîxki wîxku = to scratch (with such an object as a needle, as was done by doctors in olden times, so as to draw blood)
un wi´xsakatan = I am playing or joking with somebody (V. and J.), I am saucy to somebody (G.)
igwixsan´ta = they joke with me, or play a joke on me (V. and J.), they are saucy to me (G.)
wi´xci ha´ra a´ki = the water is frozen
wî´x tă wî´xti = to be swollen
ig wi´xtăgi = I am swollen, transimp.
wixtika´ti = elastic like a marsh, which buldges, is swollen
wilu´k ta ko´ra = "elm drink or medicine," (Gatschet gives this as the name of the slippery elm, but Johnson says it is diifferent)
wi´rax wi´ra = count, account, to count, to relate
wi´rakatani = I count, I am relating to him
wi´ran ka´xckon = What time is it?
wi´ran tu´xkusa = nine hours, nine o'clock
ugui´r'hätan = you (f.) go and ask for, demand him
ka´tantuk wi´raxkini = I remain (or dwell) somewhere
wi´cka to´xkatan = I fire a gun
wi´cka ta´hi = (f.) bow (made of hickory and acacia)
wixsan´ na´xta widjî´anan = let us climb the bluff
nukctawi´djini = "climbing on the bear"(?), water snipe (V. and J.), woodcock (G.); Creole becasse de nuits(?)
se´he widjă´ssa = at day-break, "before day mounts"
wō wotu = to make a fire
a-i wohotuni = I finish making the fire
ayi wo-u´ me´li = charcoal (V.), "black coals" (?) (see to´ra)
tapo´ku uwo´xkutan = I cover a pot, or pitcher, a cover
la´spi tco´ho ro´wa wo´xkoni = cover of tin bucket, "white metal cup cover"
ha´woxkinta = they envelop or cover him (in a cotton cloth)
ka´ku uk ri´xku wo´'hku = Whose is this box?
wo´xsuni´ wo´xsu = striped, which has lines
wo´xsu tci´kun = striped in a serpentine manner, undulating
wo´lu = to urinate (same word for all persons)
u´cki tawo´lon puskaka´tan = I distend a urine bladder
wō´ron = to teach, show, make see
woruxka´tani = I am showing, making, see, I am teaching
worulehe´ ma´lexkini = I have learned thoroughly
ro´hin tawo´ron ri = schoolhouse, "house for teaching"
wō´tū = to give to eat, to feed
kaku´to wo´ton = I serve someone while eaeting
ka´pac wo´tukiti = we feed, raise, or domesticate hens
wo´tu ci´mi = a fed, i.e., tame pigeon
ta´kuaku mu´tcui wo´yu uwata´n = the duck plunges in going in swimming
ta´niniku wo´yuku uktakuin´ta = the fish swims with its fins
ni´ni tawu´xku nini ta-uki = (m.) fishhook, fishline
wu´lin po´xtu = cut on the bias (?), oblique(?), "cut across points"
wulinda sitac = they go in "cuneus", or in a point like wild geese
ya- = prefix indicating that one person or thing reaches another from the point of view of the latter, i.e. "to arrive coming"
ya yia hia = to make, to do, cause to be
ya´xca wigia´ti = Art thou made sick?
ă´tcara ya tu = antelope, "little deer dwelling in the palmettos"
ya´axki = word used in forming numeral adverbs
yahu´ni ha´lan = picture of a monkey (J.); imprint or coinage ("melted portrait"); picture of a limb
ya´ka = crane, heron (perhaps meaning "to come" (from the south))
ya´ka ro´a ro´wa = the white or American egret; Creole egret
ya´ka me´li = great blue heron; Creole grairo (?)
hî´ran ta´yaxku = (f.) medicine for poisoning (perhaps this he hî´ran ta´yaxpu, -- see ya´xpu)
ayaxpa´yaxpa´ndăn = they go in single file, one by one
ya´xpu = to poison (there appears to be some confusion between this and ya´xku q.v.)
u´wa ta ya´xpu = "owl poison", oak way, mistletoe (?); Creole, des gee or des zee
ya´xsa = to knead, to roll flour
hut' a´yaxsa = the entire mixture, all mixed together
ya´xci yaca = sick; to be angry; pain grief
la´lixtc ya´xcina ti-a´gati = Lally thinks that she is sick
ya´xca wigia´ti = Art thou made sick?
yăm irakura = he is to wear fine clothing
o´ni yă´makura = a man who dresses well
o´ni yăma = a fashionable or well dressed man
ikya´maki = I put my fine clothes on, I plume myself
ta´-uc mi´li ya´rakati = the Red River is falling
ta´-uc mi´li ya´ratu = the Red River is low
ta-ūcăna ya´ra = the bottom of a lake (J.)
wi´ci ya´can he´kuni = lemonade, sugared beer, "sugared sour water"
yati yäti = then, now, there (cf. hin and ka)
ya = to do, or to make
u´ni ya´wa = a sorcerer, sorceress, wizard, witch, evil person
nu´xtci ya´wa malaki = a sorceress, a woman who does evil
ikya´waki = I made up my mind right away (V.?)
ukya´waki = he made up his mind right away (V.?)
hal ha´ixku yo´roni = a Tunica of another country
yo´uix katani = I stumble about and try to catch myself to keep from falling (J.)
yu- = prefix indicating that one person or thing reaches another from the point of view of the former's starting point, i.e. "to arrive going"
mir ha´yic yu´kati = the light has come above
e´yu ama´yi = the two arms, both arms
on o´yu = arm of a man
ta yumära = the wrist or bracelet
o´xcka tce´xkin ta´yuki = a cooking kettle
ta kuti ta´yikiku = a male cook
ta kuti ta´yikitc = a female cook
ta kotiu´ kic = in the hominy
yū´xtari yu´xtar = (m.) the feather (of duck, hen, etc.)
yu´nari i´unari = the jack fish (?); Creole la grande greule or "sacale"; Gatschet gives it as salmon, but is probably wrong
yu´paxta e´xsin ha-u´kin = a cold in the head
yū´ri = evil, injury, core, visible sickness
yuru yora = many, a lot of (cf. preceding stem)
yu´rukati yo´rakati = she has many (children) (G.), have more (J.)
yu´rukati = I am making a lot (V.)
